fact: Robert Johnson recorded 'Cross Road Blues' at a session in San Antonio, Texas.
Elvis Presley recorded 'That's All Right' at Sun Studio in Memphis, Tennessee.
Buddy Holly, Ritchie Valens, and The Big Bopper died in a plane crash near Clear Lake, Iowa — 'The Day the Music Died.'
The Beatles made their first appearance on The Ed Sullivan Show, watched by 73 million viewers.
Bob Dylan controversially went electric at the Newport Folk Festival.
The Beatles played their final live concert on the rooftop of Apple Corps in London.
Jimi Hendrix performed his iconic rendition of 'The Star-Spangled Banner' at Woodstock.
The Woodstock Music & Art Fair opened on Max Yasgur's dairy farm in Bethel, New York.
The Rolling Stones held their free concert at Altamont Speedway, marred by violence and a fatal stabbing.
Jimi Hendrix was found dead in London at age 27.
Janis Joplin was found dead at the Landmark Motor Hotel in Hollywood at age 27.
Jim Morrison was found dead in his bathtub in Paris at age 27.
Don McLean released 'American Pie,' an 8-minute epic referencing 'The Day the Music Died.'
David Bowie debuted his Ziggy Stardust persona at the Toby Jug pub in Tolworth, London.
Pink Floyd released 'The Dark Side of the Moon,' which spent 937 weeks on the Billboard 200.
The Ramones played their first gig at CBGB in New York City, helping launch punk rock.
Bruce Springsteen appeared on the covers of both Time and Newsweek simultaneously.
The Sex Pistols released 'Anarchy in the U.K.,' igniting the UK punk movement.
Elvis Presley was found dead at Graceland in Memphis, Tennessee, at age 42.
Saturday Night Fever soundtrack was released, fueling the disco era and becoming one of the best-selling soundtracks ever.
The Clash released 'London Calling,' widely regarded as one of the greatest albums of all time.
John Bonham, Led Zeppelin's drummer, was found dead at Jimmy Page's home, leading to the band's dissolution.
John Lennon was shot and killed outside The Dakota in New York City.
MTV launched with the broadcast of 'Video Killed the Radio Star' by The Buggles.
Michael Jackson released 'Thriller,' which became the best-selling album of all time with over 70 million copies sold.
The first compact disc (CD) was commercially released — Billy Joel's '52nd Street' in Japan.
Michael Jackson debuted the moonwalk on Motown 25: Yesterday, Today, Forever.
Marvin Gaye was shot and killed by his father in Los Angeles, one day before his 45th birthday.
Prince released 'Purple Rain,' the album and film that made him a global superstar.
Band Aid recorded 'Do They Know It's Christmas?' at SARM West Studios in London.
Live Aid concerts were held simultaneously in London and Philadelphia, raising over $125 million for Ethiopian famine relief.
Run-D.M.C. and Aerosmith released 'Walk This Way,' bridging rock and hip-hop.
The Beastie Boys released 'Licensed to Ill,' the first hip-hop album to top the Billboard 200.
U2 released 'The Joshua Tree,' selling over 25 million copies worldwide.
Public Enemy released 'It Takes a Nation of Millions to Hold Us Back,' a landmark in politically conscious hip-hop.
N.W.A released 'Straight Outta Compton,' bringing gangsta rap to national attention and drawing FBI scrutiny.
Nirvana released 'Nevermind,' which knocked Michael Jackson off the #1 Billboard spot and launched grunge into the mainstream.
Freddie Mercury of Queen died of AIDS-related bronchopneumonia in London at age 45.
Dr. Dre released 'The Chronic,' popularizing G-funk and West Coast hip-hop.
Whitney Houston's 'I Will Always Love You' became the best-selling single by a female artist at the time.
Wu-Tang Clan released 'Enter the Wu-Tang (36 Chambers),' revolutionizing East Coast hip-hop.
Kurt Cobain was found dead at his home in Seattle from a self-inflicted gunshot wound at age 27.
Notorious B.I.G. released 'Ready to Die,' widely regarded as one of the greatest hip-hop albums.
Tupac Shakur was shot in a drive-by in Las Vegas and died six days later at age 25.
The Spice Girls released 'Wannabe,' which hit #1 in 37 countries and launched the girl-power phenomenon.
The Notorious B.I.G. was shot and killed in a drive-by shooting in Los Angeles at age 24.
Radiohead released 'OK Computer,' widely regarded as one of the most influential albums of the 1990s.
Elton John performed 'Candle in the Wind 1997' at Princess Diana's funeral, and the single became the best-selling single of all time.
The first MP3 player, the MPMan F10, was released by South Korean company SaeHan Information Systems.
Lauryn Hill released 'The Miseducation of Lauryn Hill,' winning five Grammys including Album of the Year.
Napster launched, enabling free peer-to-peer music file sharing and disrupting the music industry.
Eminem released 'The Slim Shady LP,' his major-label debut that won the Grammy for Best Rap Album.
Santana's 'Supernatural' won eight Grammys, tying Michael Jackson's record for most Grammys in a single night.
Apple launched iTunes, transforming how music was purchased and consumed.
Apple launched the iPod, which would revolutionize portable music listening.
The iTunes Music Store launched, selling songs for $0.99 each.
Beyoncé released 'Crazy in Love' featuring Jay-Z, marking her solo breakout.
OutKast released 'Hey Ya!' which became one of the most iconic songs of the 2000s.
Kanye West released 'The College Dropout,' his debut album that reinvented hip-hop production.
Green Day released 'American Idiot,' a punk rock opera that revitalized the genre.
YouTube launched, fundamentally changing music discovery and consumption.
Johnny Cash's posthumous 'Hurt' music video won the CMA Award for Music Video of the Year.
Amy Winehouse released 'Back to Black,' blending soul, jazz, and R&B and winning five Grammys.
Spotify launched in Sweden, pioneering the music streaming model.
Michael Jackson died of cardiac arrest in Los Angeles at age 50.
Taylor Swift's 'Fearless' won Album of the Year at the Grammys, making her the youngest artist to win the award at 20.
Kanye West interrupted Taylor Swift's acceptance speech at the MTV VMAs.
Amy Winehouse was found dead at her London home at age 27.
Adele's '21' became the best-selling album of the 21st century with over 31 million copies.
PSY's 'Gangnam Style' became the first YouTube video to reach 1 billion views.
Whitney Houston was found dead in a hotel bathtub in Beverly Hills at age 48.
Daft Punk released 'Random Access Memories,' featuring 'Get Lucky,' which won Album of the Year at the Grammys.
Beyoncé surprise-released her self-titled visual album on iTunes with no prior announcement.
Pharrell Williams' 'Happy' topped charts in 23 countries and was nominated for the Academy Award for Best Original Song.
Apple acquired Beats Electronics for $3 billion, Apple's largest acquisition at the time.
Kendrick Lamar released 'To Pimp a Butterfly,' a critically acclaimed album blending hip-hop, jazz, funk, and spoken word.
Adele's '25' sold 3.38 million copies in its first week in the US, shattering sales records.
David Bowie released 'Blackstar' and died two days later of liver cancer at age 69.
Prince was found dead at his Paisley Park home in Minnesota at age 57.
Beyoncé released 'Lemonade' as an HBO visual album, a cultural landmark exploring Black womanhood.
Bob Dylan was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature for 'having created new poetic expressions within the great American song tradition.'
Luis Fonsi and Daddy Yankee's 'Despacito' became the most-streamed song in history and topped charts in 47 countries.
Chester Bennington of Linkin Park was found dead at age 41.
Kendrick Lamar's 'DAMN.' won the Pulitzer Prize for Music, the first non-classical or jazz work to receive the honor.
BTS became the first K-pop group to top the Billboard Hot 100 with 'Dynamite.'
Taylor Swift released 'Folklore' as a surprise album during the COVID-19 pandemic, winning Album of the Year.
The Weeknd performed the Super Bowl LV halftime show.
Olivia Rodrigo's 'drivers license' broke Spotify's record for most streams in a single day.
Silk Sonic (Bruno Mars & Anderson .Paak) won Record of the Year and Song of the Year at the Grammys for 'Leave the Door Open.'
Taylor Swift's 'Midnights' broke the record for most-streamed album in a single day on Spotify.
Beyoncé's 'Renaissance' World Tour became the highest-grossing tour by a female artist.
The Beatles released 'Now and Then,' their final song, using AI to isolate John Lennon's voice from a demo.
Taylor Swift's Eras Tour became the first tour to gross over $1 billion.
Robert Johnson was born in Hazlehurst, Mississippi, one of the most influential blues musicians of all time.
W.C. Handy published 'Memphis Blues,' one of the first blues compositions to be published as sheet music.
The Original Dixieland Jass Band recorded 'Livery Stable Blues,' the first jazz recording ever made.
Mamie Smith recorded 'Crazy Blues,' the first blues vocal recording by an African American artist, selling 75,000 copies in its first month.
Louis Armstrong joined King Oliver's Creole Jazz Band in Chicago, a pivotal moment in jazz history.
Bessie Smith recorded 'Downhearted Blues,' which sold 780,000 copies and established her as the 'Empress of the Blues.'
The Grand Ole Opry radio show debuted as the WSM Barn Dance in Nashville, Tennessee.
Ralph Peer recorded the Carter Family and Jimmie Rodgers in Bristol, Tennessee — the 'Big Bang of Country Music.'
Duke Ellington began his legendary residency at the Cotton Club in Harlem, New York.
Bing Crosby recorded 'White Christmas,' which became the best-selling single of all time with over 50 million copies.
Billboard published its first 'Music Popularity Chart,' the precursor to the Hot 100.
Frank Sinatra caused mass hysteria at the Paramount Theatre in New York, an early example of teen idol mania.
Muddy Waters recorded 'Rollin' Stone' in Chicago, the song that inspired the name of both the band and the magazine.
Alan Freed hosted the Moondog Coronation Ball in Cleveland, widely considered the first major rock and roll concert. A near-riot broke out.
Sam Phillips recorded what many consider the first rock and roll record: 'Rocket 88' by Jackie Brenston and His Delta Cats at Sun Studio.
Bill Haley & His Comets released 'Rock Around the Clock,' which became a rock and roll anthem after appearing in the film 'Blackboard Jungle.'
Chuck Berry recorded 'Maybellene,' blending country and R&B into a definitive rock and roll sound.
Little Richard recorded 'Tutti Frutti' at J&M Studio in New Orleans.
Elvis Presley appeared on The Ed Sullivan Show for the first time, watched by approximately 60 million viewers.
Jerry Lee Lewis recorded 'Great Balls of Fire' at Sun Studio in Memphis.
Berry Gordy Jr. founded Motown Records in Detroit, Michigan.
The Beatles were rejected by Decca Records, with the executive saying 'guitar groups are on their way out.'
The Beatles released their debut album 'Please Please Me,' recorded in a single day at Abbey Road Studios.
Bob Dylan released 'The Freewheelin' Bob Dylan,' featuring 'Blowin' in the Wind,' which became a civil rights anthem.
Martin Luther King Jr. led the March on Washington, where 'We Shall Overcome' became the anthem of the civil rights movement.
The Rolling Stones released their self-titled debut album in the UK.
The Beatles held the top five positions on the Billboard Hot 100 simultaneously — a feat never repeated.
Bob Dylan released 'Highway 61 Revisited,' featuring 'Like a Rolling Stone,' voted the greatest song of all time by Rolling Stone magazine.
The Beach Boys released 'Pet Sounds,' which inspired The Beatles' 'Sgt. Pepper.'
The Beatles released 'Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band,' widely considered one of the most important albums ever made.
The Monterey Pop Festival featured Jimi Hendrix setting his guitar on fire and Janis Joplin's breakthrough performance.
Otis Redding died in a plane crash in Madison, Wisconsin, at age 26. '(Sittin' On) The Dock of the Bay' was released posthumously.
James Brown performed a legendary concert at the Boston Garden the night after Martin Luther King Jr.'s assassination, helping to prevent riots.
Johnny Cash recorded 'At Folsom Prison,' a landmark live album performed for inmates.
Led Zeppelin released their self-titled debut album, helping define heavy metal and hard rock.
The Who released 'Tommy,' the first major rock opera.
Miles Davis released 'Bitches Brew,' fusing jazz with rock and creating jazz fusion.
Simon & Garfunkel's 'Bridge Over Troubled Water' topped charts in over a dozen countries.
Marvin Gaye released 'What's Going On,' a concept album addressing social issues that Motown initially refused to release.
Led Zeppelin released 'Led Zeppelin IV,' containing 'Stairway to Heaven,' the most requested FM radio song in history.
Stevie Wonder released 'Innervisions,' beginning his classic period and winning the Grammy for Album of the Year.
ABBA won the Eurovision Song Contest with 'Waterloo,' launching their international career.
Bob Marley and the Wailers released 'Natty Dread,' helping bring reggae to a worldwide audience.
Kraftwerk released 'Autobahn,' pioneering electronic music and influencing synth-pop, techno, and hip-hop.
Patti Smith released 'Horses,' a landmark album bridging punk rock and poetry.
The Sex Pistols performed their infamous interview with Bill Grundy on Thames Television, shocking the UK.
Donna Summer released 'I Feel Love,' produced by Giorgio Moroder, revolutionizing electronic dance music.
Bob Marley was shot at his home in Jamaica but survived and performed at the Smile Jamaica concert two days later.
The Bee Gees' Saturday Night Fever soundtrack became one of the best-selling albums ever with over 40 million copies.
The Sugarhill Gang released 'Rapper's Delight,' the first hip-hop single to reach the Top 40 on the Billboard Hot 100.
Joy Division's Ian Curtis died by suicide at age 23, leading the remaining members to form New Order.
Bob Marley died of melanoma in Miami at age 36.
Grandmaster Flash and the Furious Five released 'The Message,' one of the first hip-hop songs to deliver social commentary.
The first CD players went on sale in Japan — the Sony CDP-101.
Madonna released her self-titled debut album, beginning her rise as the 'Queen of Pop.'
Michael Jackson's 'Thriller' music video premiered on MTV, a 14-minute mini-film directed by John Landis.
Metallica released 'Ride the Lightning,' establishing them as a leading thrash metal band.
We Are the World was recorded by USA for Africa at A&M Studios in Hollywood.
Queen performed their legendary 21-minute set at Live Aid in Wembley Stadium, widely considered the greatest live performance in rock history.
Whitney Houston released her self-titled debut album, which produced three #1 singles.
Paul Simon released 'Graceland,' incorporating South African music and facing an anti-apartheid cultural boycott controversy.
The first Rock and Roll Hall of Fame induction ceremony took place, inducting Chuck Berry, James Brown, Ray Charles, Sam Cooke, Fats Domino, and others.
Guns N' Roses released 'Appetite for Destruction,' which became the best-selling debut album of all time in the US.
DJ Scott La Rock of Boogie Down Productions was shot and killed in the Bronx at age 25.
Tracy Chapman released her self-titled debut album, featuring 'Fast Car,' which crossed folk into the mainstream.
De La Soul released '3 Feet High and Rising,' pioneering alternative hip-hop with its eclectic sampling.
The Stone Roses released their self-titled debut album, a cornerstone of the Madchester movement.
MC Hammer released 'U Can't Touch This,' one of the first hip-hop songs to achieve massive pop crossover success.
Sinéad O'Connor's cover of 'Nothing Compares 2 U' topped charts worldwide.
A Tribe Called Quest released 'The Low End Theory,' blending jazz and hip-hop.
My Bloody Valentine released 'Loveless,' the definitive shoegaze album, reportedly costing over £250,000 to produce.
Pearl Jam released 'Ten,' which along with Nirvana's 'Nevermind' defined the grunge movement.
Tupac Shakur released 'All Eyez on Me,' the first double-disc studio album in hip-hop history.
The Fugees released 'The Score,' which sold over 22 million copies worldwide.
Buena Vista Social Club was recorded in Havana, Cuba, reviving interest in traditional Cuban music worldwide.
Radiohead released 'In Rainbows' as a pay-what-you-want download, pioneering a new distribution model.
Fela Kuti, the creator of Afrobeat, died in Lagos, Nigeria, at age 58.
The Dixie Chicks' Natalie Maines criticized President George W. Bush at a London concert, sparking a massive backlash.
Ray Charles died in Beverly Hills at age 73.
Arctic Monkeys became the fastest-selling debut act in UK chart history with 'Whatever People Say I Am, That's What I'm Not.'
James Brown, the 'Godfather of Soul,' died on Christmas Day in Atlanta at age 73.
Kanye West released '808s & Heartbreak,' pioneering Auto-Tune in hip-hop and influencing Drake, Kid Cudi, and the emo-rap wave.
Lady Gaga released 'The Fame,' launching her as one of pop's biggest stars with 'Just Dance' and 'Poker Face.'
Susan Boyle's audition on Britain's Got Talent went viral, becoming one of the most-watched YouTube videos of all time.
Arcade Fire's 'The Suburbs' won Album of the Year at the Grammys, a surprise indie rock victory.
Frank Ocean released 'Channel Orange,' shortly after publicly coming out, a landmark moment for LGBTQ+ representation in hip-hop/R&B.
Streaming revenue surpassed physical sales for the first time in the US music industry.
Leonard Cohen died in Los Angeles at age 82, just days after releasing 'You Want It Darker.'
The Academy Awards mistakenly announced La La Land as Best Picture instead of Moonlight in the most infamous Oscar error ever — La La Land's soundtrack had already won multiple awards.
XXXTentacion was shot and killed in Deerfield Beach, Florida, at age 20.
Lil Nas X's 'Old Town Road' spent a record-breaking 19 weeks at #1 on the Billboard Hot 100.
Billie Eilish, at age 18, swept the four main Grammy categories (Record, Album, Song of the Year, Best New Artist).
The COVID-19 pandemic shut down live music globally, leading to the rise of virtual concerts and livestreamed performances.
Travis Scott's Astroworld Festival crowd crush in Houston killed 10 people.
Foo Fighters drummer Taylor Hawkins was found dead in his hotel room in Bogotá, Colombia, at age 50.
Johann Sebastian Bach was born in Eisenach, Germany.
Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart was born in Salzburg, Austria.
Ludwig van Beethoven was baptized in Bonn, Germany (exact birth date unknown, likely December 16).
Beethoven's Symphony No. 9 premiered in Vienna, featuring 'Ode to Joy' — he was almost completely deaf.
Frédéric Chopin gave his final public concert in London.
Thomas Edison invented the phonograph, the first device capable of recording and reproducing sound.
Enrico Caruso made his first phonograph recordings, helping establish recorded music as a commercial medium.
Igor Stravinsky's 'The Rite of Spring' premiered in Paris, causing a near-riot due to its radical composition.
The first commercial radio broadcast of music occurred on KDKA Pittsburgh.
George Gershwin's 'Rhapsody in Blue' premiered at Aeolian Hall in New York, blending jazz and classical.
Robert Moog demonstrated the Moog synthesizer at the AES convention, changing electronic music forever.
Wendy Carlos released 'Switched-On Bach,' a classical album performed entirely on the Moog synthesizer, winning three Grammys.
Giorgio Moroder produced Donna Summer's 'Love to Love You Baby,' a 17-minute disco track that pioneered electronic dance production.
The Roland TR-808 drum machine was released, becoming one of the most influential instruments in hip-hop, electronic, and pop music.
Afrika Bambaataa and the Soulsonic Force released 'Planet Rock,' fusing electronic music with hip-hop using the TR-808.
The first house music tracks emerged from Chicago, with Frankie Knuckles DJing at The Warehouse.
Juan Atkins released 'No UFOs' under the name Model 500, considered one of the first Detroit techno records.
The first acid house parties emerged in the UK, starting the Second Summer of Love rave movement.
The Prodigy released 'The Fat of the Land,' debuting at #1 in 23 countries and bringing electronic music to the mainstream.
Daft Punk released 'Homework,' featuring 'Around the World' and 'Da Funk,' establishing French house.
Deadmau5 began performing in his signature mouse helmet, becoming one of EDM's most recognizable figures.
Skrillex released the 'Scary Monsters and Nice Sprites' EP, bringing dubstep to mainstream America.
The first Electric Daisy Carnival (EDC) was held at the Los Angeles Coliseum.
Avicii's 'Levels' was released, becoming an EDM anthem.
Avicii was found dead in Muscat, Oman, at age 28.
Hank Williams died in the back seat of his Cadillac on the way to a concert in Canton, Ohio, at age 29.
Patsy Cline recorded 'Crazy,' written by Willie Nelson, one of the most iconic country songs ever.
Patsy Cline died in a plane crash near Camden, Tennessee, at age 30.
Johnny Cash proposed to June Carter on stage during a performance in London, Ontario.
Willie Nelson released 'Red Headed Stranger,' a concept album that defined outlaw country.
Dolly Parton released '9 to 5,' which became a feminist anthem and topped both country and pop charts.
Garth Brooks released 'No Fences,' which became the best-selling country album of all time.
Shania Twain released 'Come On Over,' which became the best-selling album by a female artist with over 40 million copies.
Johnny Cash died in Nashville at age 71, just four months after the death of his wife June Carter Cash.
Carrie Underwood won American Idol Season 4, launching one of country music's biggest careers.
Ray Charles' 'Modern Sounds in Country and Western Music' became one of the first major crossover albums, blending R&B and country.
Sam Cooke was shot and killed at the Hacienda Motel in Los Angeles at age 33 under disputed circumstances.
Aretha Franklin recorded 'Respect' at Atlantic Studios in New York, transforming Otis Redding's song into a feminist and civil rights anthem.
Stevie Wonder signed with Motown at age 11 as 'Little Stevie Wonder.'
Al Green released 'Let's Stay Together,' one of the defining songs of 1970s soul.
Donna Summer released 'Last Dance,' winning the Academy Award for Best Original Song.
Michael Jackson's 'Off the Wall' was released, produced by Quincy Jones, bridging disco and pop.
Prince released '1999,' featuring the title track that became a New Year's anthem worldwide.
Tina Turner released 'Private Dancer,' her solo comeback that sold over 20 million copies.
R. Kelly released 'Trapped in the Closet,' an ambitious hip-hopera spanning 33 chapters.
D'Angelo released 'Voodoo,' a neo-soul masterpiece that won the Grammy for Best R&B Album.
Aretha Franklin died in Detroit at age 76.
Tina Turner died at her home in Küsnacht, Switzerland, at age 83.
Black Sabbath released their self-titled debut album, widely considered the birth of heavy metal.
Iron Maiden released 'The Number of the Beast,' a defining album of the New Wave of British Heavy Metal.
Metallica released 'Master of Puppets,' widely regarded as one of the greatest metal albums of all time.
Cliff Burton, Metallica's bassist, died in a bus accident in Sweden at age 24.
Slayer released 'Reign in Blood,' a 29-minute thrash metal masterpiece.
Pantera released 'Vulgar Display of Power,' defining groove metal.
Nine Inch Nails released 'The Downward Spiral,' blending industrial and metal into a landmark album.
Tool released 'Lateralus,' a progressive metal album incorporating Fibonacci sequences in its rhythms.
Dimebag Darrell of Pantera was shot and killed on stage in Columbus, Ohio, at age 38.
Lemmy Kilmister, Motörhead's frontman, died in Los Angeles at age 70.
Bob Dylan released 'The Times They Are a-Changin',' a protest album that became a soundtrack for social change.
Joni Mitchell released 'Blue,' often cited as the greatest singer-songwriter album of all time.
Nick Drake died of an antidepressant overdose at age 26, only gaining widespread recognition decades later.
Woody Guthrie wrote 'This Land Is Your Land' as a response to Irving Berlin's 'God Bless America.'
Pete Seeger was cited for contempt of Congress after refusing to answer questions about his political associations before the House Un-American Activities Committee.
Joan Baez performed at the March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom, singing 'We Shall Overcome.'
Leonard Cohen released 'Songs of Leonard Cohen,' his debut album featuring 'Suzanne.'
Jeff Buckley drowned in the Wolf River in Memphis, Tennessee, at age 30.
Elliott Smith died of stab wounds in Los Angeles at age 34 under disputed circumstances.
Bon Iver's 'For Emma, Forever Ago' was recorded in a remote Wisconsin cabin and launched Justin Vernon's career.
Mumford & Sons' 'Babel' debuted at #1 in the UK and US, becoming the fastest-selling album of 2012.
The first Lollapalooza festival was held as a farewell tour for Jane's Addiction.
The first Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival was held in Indio, California.
The first Glastonbury Festival was held at Worthy Farm in Somerset, England, with T. Rex headlining. Tickets cost £1.
The first Bonnaroo Music Festival was held in Manchester, Tennessee.
The Isle of Wight Festival drew an estimated 600,000 people, the largest gathering of its kind at the time.
Ravi Shankar performed at the Monterey Pop Festival, introducing Indian classical music to a Western rock audience.
Fela Kuti's 'Zombie' was released, a scathing critique of the Nigerian military that led to a violent raid on his compound.
Bob Marley and the Wailers released 'Exodus,' named the best album of the 20th century by Time magazine.
Peter Tosh was murdered in his home in Kingston, Jamaica, at age 42.
Youssou N'Dour and Neneh Cherry released '7 Seconds,' a global hit blending Afro-pop and Western pop.
Buena Vista Social Club's album won the Grammy for Best Tropical Latin Performance.
Ricky Martin performed 'La Copa de la Vida' at the Grammys, sparking the Latin pop explosion of 1999.
Shakira released 'Laundry Service,' her first English-language album, selling over 20 million copies.
Daddy Yankee released 'Barrio Fino,' featuring 'Gasolina,' which brought reggaeton to global prominence.
BTS debuted with the single '2 Cool 4 Skool,' beginning their rise as the biggest K-pop act in history.
PSY released 'Gangnam Style,' the first K-pop song to become a truly global phenomenon.
BLACKPINK's 'DDU-DU DDU-DU' became the most-viewed music video by a K-pop group.
Seo Taiji and Boys debuted on a Korean TV talent show, founding modern K-pop.
BoA became the first Korean solo artist to top Japan's Oricon chart, paving the way for K-pop in Japan.
Wonder Girls' 'Nobody' became the first K-pop song to chart on the Billboard Hot 100.
BTS spoke at the United Nations General Assembly, the first K-pop group to do so.
BLACKPINK became the first K-pop group to headline Coachella.
The LP (long-playing) vinyl record was introduced by Columbia Records, playing at 33⅓ RPM.
RCA Victor introduced the 45 RPM single, creating the format war with Columbia's LP.
The Philips compact cassette was introduced at the Berlin Radio Show.
The Sony Walkman was released in Japan, making portable music listening a cultural phenomenon.
The audio CD format was jointly developed by Sony and Philips.
The Fraunhofer Institute received a patent for the MP3 audio format.
The Diamond Rio PMP300, one of the first commercially successful MP3 players, was released.
Napster was shut down after losing its legal battle with the RIAA.
Apple launched the iTunes Music Store with 200,000 songs, selling 1 million songs in its first week.
SoundCloud launched in Berlin, becoming a major platform for independent music distribution.
Spotify launched in the US after three years of European-only availability.
Vinyl records outsold CDs in the US for the first time since 1987.
Apple discontinued the iPod Touch, ending the iPod product line after 21 years.
Tidal launched as a high-fidelity, artist-owned streaming platform backed by Jay-Z, Beyoncé, and others.
The first Grammy Awards ceremony was held at the Beverly Hilton Hotel. Domenico Modugno won Record of the Year for 'Volare.'
Stevie Wonder won Album of the Year at the Grammys for three out of four consecutive years ('Innervisions,' 'Fulfillingness' First Finale,' 'Songs in the Key of Life').
Christopher Cross swept all four major Grammy categories with his debut album — the first and only time until Billie Eilish in 2020.
Jethro Tull controversially won the first Grammy for Best Hard Rock/Metal Performance over Metallica.
Bob Dylan won the Grammy for Album of the Year for 'Time Out of Mind,' his first Album of the Year win.
Outkast's 'Speakerboxxx/The Love Below' won Album of the Year, the second hip-hop album to win.
Herbie Hancock's 'River: The Joni Letters' won Album of the Year, a surprise jazz album victory.
Beyoncé won the most Grammys ever by a single artist, surpassing Georg Solti's record of 31.
The Beatles appeared on The Ed Sullivan Show for the second time, drawing 70 million viewers.
Dick Clark's American Bandstand first aired nationally on ABC, bringing rock and roll to American living rooms.
The Buggles' 'Video Killed the Radio Star' was the first music video played on MTV.
Michael Jackson's 'Beat It' music video premiered, featuring Eddie Van Halen's guitar solo.
Bob Geldof organized the Live 8 concerts across nine cities worldwide to coincide with the G8 summit.
Nirvana recorded their iconic MTV Unplugged session in New York.
Jay-Z and Linkin Park released 'Collision Course,' a mashup EP blending hip-hop and rock.
The White Stripes' 'Seven Nation Army' riff became the most popular sports chant in the world.
Miles Davis released 'Kind of Blue,' the best-selling jazz album of all time.
John Coltrane released 'A Love Supreme,' a spiritual masterpiece considered one of jazz's greatest achievements.
Charlie Parker died in New York at age 34. The graffiti 'Bird Lives' appeared across the city.
Billie Holiday was arrested for narcotics possession and subsequently barred from performing in New York City venues with a cabaret card.
Billie Holiday died in New York at age 44 while under arrest for heroin possession on her deathbed.
Thelonious Monk appeared on the cover of Time magazine, only the third jazz musician to do so.
Dave Brubeck's 'Time Out' album was released, featuring 'Take Five' — the first jazz single to sell a million copies.
Ella Fitzgerald and Louis Armstrong recorded 'Ella and Louis,' one of the greatest vocal jazz albums.
Louis Armstrong's 'What a Wonderful World' was released, initially a flop in the US but a massive hit in the UK.
Dexter Gordon starred in the film 'Round Midnight,' earning an Oscar nomination — the first jazz musician to do so.
Wynton Marsalis won the Pulitzer Prize for Music for 'Blood on the Fields,' the first jazz composition to receive the honor.
Tommy Johnson claimed to have sold his soul to the devil at a crossroads in Mississippi, a legend later attributed to Robert Johnson.
B.B. King named his guitar 'Lucille' after risking his life to save it from a fire at a dance hall in Twist, Arkansas.
Muddy Waters performed at the Newport Jazz Festival, exposing a white audience to electric Chicago blues.
Howlin' Wolf appeared on Shindig!, one of the first times an authentic blues artist appeared on national TV.
B.B. King and Eric Clapton recorded 'Riding with the King,' bridging blues and rock for a new generation.
B.B. King died in Las Vegas at age 89.
Rage Against the Machine's 'Killing in the Name' topped the UK Christmas chart in 2009 after a viral Facebook campaign against X Factor.
Tupac Shakur's hologram appeared at Coachella alongside Snoop Dogg and Dr. Dre.
Fleetwood Mac's 'Rumours' was released during the band members' relationship breakdowns, selling over 40 million copies.
Talking Heads released 'Remain in Light,' blending post-punk with Afrobeat in a groundbreaking fusion.
R.E.M. released 'Murmur,' helping define the alternative rock genre.
The Smiths released 'The Queen Is Dead,' a cornerstone of indie rock.
Pixies released 'Doolittle,' influencing Nirvana and the alternative rock explosion.
Massive Attack released 'Blue Lines,' essentially creating the trip-hop genre.
Portishead released 'Dummy,' winning the Mercury Prize and defining trip-hop alongside Massive Attack.
Oasis released '(What's the Story) Morning Glory?,' selling over 22 million copies and fueling the Britpop movement.
The Oasis vs. Blur rivalry culminated in both bands releasing singles on the same day. Blur's 'Country House' outsold Oasis's 'Roll with It.'
Björk released 'Post,' continuing her pioneering blend of electronic and art pop.
OK Go released the treadmill music video for 'Here It Goes Again,' one of the first viral music videos on YouTube.
Gorillaz released their self-titled debut album, becoming the most successful virtual band in music history.
The Black Keys released 'Brothers,' winning three Grammy Awards and bringing blues-rock back to the mainstream.
Childish Gambino's 'This Is America' music video premiered, becoming a viral cultural phenomenon about gun violence and racism.
Tyler, the Creator won the Grammy for Best Rap Album for 'Igor,' a genre-bending neo-soul and synth-pop record.
Bad Bunny became the most-streamed artist on Spotify for three consecutive years (2020-2022).
Drake released 'Scorpion,' with every track charting on the Billboard Hot 100 simultaneously.
Cardi B's 'Bodak Yellow' hit #1, making her the first solo female rapper to top the Hot 100 since Lauryn Hill in 1998.
Chance the Rapper's 'Coloring Book' became the first streaming-only album to win a Grammy.
Post Malone broke the record for most weeks on the Billboard Hot 100 with 'Circles' at 39 weeks.
Lizzo's 'Truth Hurts' reached #1 on the Billboard Hot 100 two years after its initial release, boosted by a TikTok trend.
The Weeknd's 'Blinding Lights' became the #1 song on the Billboard Hot 100 all-time chart.
SZA's 'SOS' debuted at #1 and spent 10 weeks atop the Billboard 200.
Bob Dylan's entire songwriting catalog was sold to Universal Music Publishing for an estimated $300 million.
Bruce Springsteen sold his music catalog to Sony for approximately $500 million, the largest deal for a single artist's catalog.
AC/DC's 'Back in Black' was released as a tribute to late vocalist Bon Scott, becoming the second best-selling album of all time.
Queen's 'Bohemian Rhapsody' topped the UK charts for nine weeks, defying radio conventions with its operatic structure.
The Eagles' 'Hotel California' was released, becoming one of the best-selling albums in US history.
Michael Jackson purchased the ATV music catalog containing most of The Beatles' songs for $47.5 million.
Sinéad O'Connor tore up a photo of Pope John Paul II on Saturday Night Live, sparking massive controversy.
Milli Vanilli were stripped of their Grammy Award after it was revealed they did not sing on their album.
Ozzy Osbourne bit the head off a live bat thrown on stage in Des Moines, Iowa.
The Who's Keith Moon died of an overdose in London at age 32.
Phil Spector developed the 'Wall of Sound' production technique at Gold Star Studios in Los Angeles.
Brian Wilson suffered a nervous breakdown during the recording of 'Smile,' which remained unreleased for 37 years.
The Velvet Underground released 'The Velvet Underground & Nico,' produced by Andy Warhol — commercially unsuccessful but enormously influential.
ABBA's 'Dancing Queen' topped charts worldwide, becoming one of the most recognizable pop songs ever.
Blondie's 'Rapture' became the first #1 single to feature rap vocals.
The Police released 'Synchronicity,' featuring 'Every Breath You Take,' their biggest commercial success.
George Michael released 'Faith,' which sold over 25 million copies and established him as a solo superstar.
Nirvana's Kurt Cobain and Courtney Love's relationship became tabloid fodder, exemplifying '90s grunge celebrity.
TLC's 'CrazySexyCool' became the best-selling album by an American girl group.
Destiny's Child released 'The Writing's on the Wall,' featuring 'Say My Name' and establishing Beyoncé as a star.
Norah Jones' 'Come Away with Me' won eight Grammys, including Album of the Year.
Amy Winehouse refused to attend the Grammys due to visa issues but accepted five awards via satellite from London.
Lorde's 'Royals' topped the Billboard Hot 100, making her the youngest solo artist to do so since Tiffany in 1987.
Stormzy headlined Glastonbury, the first Black British solo artist to do so.
Wiley released 'Treddin' on Thin Ice,' considered the foundational grime track.
Skepta won the Mercury Prize for 'Konnichiwa,' legitimizing grime as a major UK genre.
Eminem's 'The Marshall Mathers LP' sold 1.76 million copies in its first week, a record at the time.
50 Cent's 'Get Rich or Die Tryin'' debuted at #1, selling 872,000 copies in its first four days.
Jay-Z released 'The Blueprint' on September 11, 2001, a date that overshadowed the release of one of hip-hop's greatest albums.
Missy Elliott became the first female rapper inducted into the Songwriters Hall of Fame.
Nas released 'Illmatic,' widely regarded as the greatest hip-hop album of all time.
DJ Kool Herc hosted a back-to-school party at 1520 Sedgwick Avenue in the Bronx, widely regarded as the birth of hip-hop.
Run-D.M.C. became the first hip-hop group to appear on the cover of Rolling Stone.
Salt-N-Pepa's 'Push It' became one of the first female hip-hop songs to cross over to pop radio.
MC Lyte released 'Lyte as a Rock,' the first solo hip-hop album by a female rapper.
Tupac and Biggie's East Coast-West Coast rivalry dominated hip-hop, ending in both their murders.
Kanye West declared 'George Bush doesn't care about Black people' on live television during a Hurricane Katrina benefit.
Drake released 'Take Care,' popularizing emotional vulnerability in hip-hop.
Kendrick Lamar released 'good kid, m.A.A.d city,' a narrative album about growing up in Compton.
Cardi B became the first woman to have five simultaneous top-10 entries on the Billboard Hot 100.
Nipsey Hussle was shot and killed outside his Marathon Clothing store in Los Angeles at age 33.
J Dilla released 'Donuts' from his hospital bed, three days before dying of a rare blood disease at age 32.
Eminem performed 'Lose Yourself' at the Oscars 17 years after winning Best Original Song, his first-ever Oscar performance.
Ice-T formed Body Count and released 'Cop Killer,' sparking a national controversy over censorship.
The Notorious B.I.G.'s 'Life After Death' was released 16 days after his murder, debuting at #1.
Missy Elliott released 'Supa Dupa Fly,' produced by Timbaland, redefining hip-hop production with futuristic beats.
Kanye West was in a near-fatal car accident. He recorded 'Through the Wire' with his jaw wired shut.
Dolly Parton declined a Rock and Roll Hall of Fame nomination, saying she hadn't 'earned that right.'
The Weeknd's Super Bowl LV halftime show cost a reported $7 million out of his own pocket.
SoundCloud rap emerged as a movement, with artists like Lil Uzi Vert, XXXTentacion, and Juice WRLD gaining fame.
Juice WRLD died of an accidental drug overdose at Chicago's Midway Airport at age 21.
Mac Miller died of an accidental overdose in Los Angeles at age 26.
Selena Quintanilla was shot and killed by the president of her fan club in Corpus Christi, Texas, at age 23.
Carlos Santana's performance at Woodstock launched his career, particularly his rendition of 'Soul Sacrifice.'
Gloria Estefan's tour bus was hit by a semi-truck, breaking her back. She recovered and returned to touring.
Jennifer Lopez released 'On the 6,' bridging Latin pop and mainstream American pop.
Rosalía released 'El Mal Querer,' blending flamenco with electronic production and winning international acclaim.
Bad Bunny's 'Un Verano Sin Ti' became the most-streamed album of 2022 on Spotify.
Héctor Lavoe, the 'King of Salsa,' died in New York at age 46.
Celia Cruz, the 'Queen of Salsa,' died in Fort Lee, New Jersey, at age 77.
George Harrison organized the Concert for Bangladesh at Madison Square Garden, the first major benefit concert.
Elton John publicly came out as bisexual in a Rolling Stone interview.
Freddie Mercury's vocal range was reported to span nearly four octaves, from bass low F to soprano high F.
Queen performed at the first Rock in Rio festival before 250,000 people in Rio de Janeiro.
Nelson Mandela's 70th Birthday Tribute concert was held at Wembley Stadium, broadcast to 600 million viewers in 67 countries.
Tiananmen Square protesters played Cui Jian's 'Nothing to My Name,' making it an anthem of the Chinese democracy movement.
The Berlin Wall fell, and Pink Floyd's Roger Waters performed 'The Wall' at the Brandenburg Gate to 350,000 people.
Rage Against the Machine protested outside the Democratic National Convention and were dispersed by police.
Green Day's Billie Joe Armstrong had a meltdown on stage at the iHeartRadio Festival, leading to his rehab stint.
A$AP Rocky was arrested and detained in Sweden for assault, drawing international attention and diplomatic intervention.
Thomas Bangalter and Guy-Manuel de Homem-Christo formed Daft Punk in Paris.
Daft Punk announced their breakup via a video titled 'Epilogue' after 28 years together.
The Verve released 'Bitter Sweet Symphony,' sampling The Rolling Stones' orchestral version and losing all royalties in a lawsuit.
The Rolling Stones' songwriting royalties for 'Bitter Sweet Symphony' were returned to The Verve's Richard Ashcroft after 22 years.
Kate Bush's 'Running Up That Hill' reached #1 in several countries 37 years after release, boosted by its use in 'Stranger Things.'
Fleetwood Mac's 'Dreams' re-entered the charts after a TikTok video of a man skateboarding while drinking cranberry juice went viral.
John Cage's '4′33″' was first performed by David Tudor at Woodstock, New York — four minutes and thirty-three seconds of silence.
Philip Glass's 'Einstein on the Beach' premiered at the Avignon Festival in France, lasting five hours with no intermission.
The Three Tenors (Pavarotti, Domingo, Carreras) performed at the 1990 FIFA World Cup in Rome, selling millions of albums.
Andrea Bocelli's 'Con te partirò' became one of the best-selling singles of all time.
Gustavo Dudamel became the youngest conductor to lead the Los Angeles Philharmonic at age 28.
Spotify reported having 100 million paid subscribers worldwide.
Taylor Swift pulled her entire catalog from Spotify in a dispute over streaming royalties.
Taylor Swift returned her catalog to Spotify after three years.
Taylor Swift began re-recording her first six albums after Scooter Braun acquired her masters.
Taylor Swift's 'Fearless (Taylor's Version)' became the first re-recorded album to debut at #1 on the Billboard 200.
AI-generated song 'Heart on My Sleeve' mimicking Drake and The Weeknd went viral and was pulled from streaming platforms.
Grimes announced that anyone could use AI to create songs with her voice and split royalties 50/50.
Rick Astley's 'Never Gonna Give You Up' became an internet meme ('Rickrolling'), gaining hundreds of millions of new views.
Toto's 'Africa' experienced a massive viral resurgence, leading to Weezer recording a cover that hit #1.
Soft Cell's 'Tainted Love' spent 43 weeks on the Billboard Hot 100, the longest run at that time.
Depeche Mode released 'Violator,' a synth-pop masterpiece featuring 'Personal Jesus' and 'Enjoy the Silence.'
Aphex Twin released 'Selected Ambient Works 85-92,' a foundational album for ambient electronic music.
The Chemical Brothers released 'Dig Your Own Hole,' a landmark big beat album.
Fatboy Slim played to 250,000 people on Brighton Beach, causing crowd safety concerns and a review of public event permits.
Burial released 'Untrue,' a genre-defining dubstep album, while remaining anonymous for years.
Tame Impala's 'Currents' bridged psychedelic rock and synth-pop, becoming one of the most influential albums of the 2010s.
Paramore's 'Decode' was featured on the Twilight soundtrack, introducing the band to a massive new audience.
My Chemical Romance released 'The Black Parade,' the defining emo album of the 2000s.
Fall Out Boy released 'From Under the Cork Tree,' selling over 7 million copies and leading the pop-punk revival.
Twenty One Pilots' 'Blurryface' became the first album where every track received at least a gold certification.
Elvis Presley's Aloha from Hawaii concert was broadcast via satellite to over 1 billion viewers worldwide.
The Grateful Dead performed 2,318 concerts before Jerry Garcia's death, pioneering the jam band genre.
Jerry Garcia died in his sleep at a rehabilitation clinic in Forest Knolls, California, at age 53.
Phish performed their New Year's Eve concert, flying over the audience in a giant hot dog.
Frank Sinatra recorded 'My Way,' written by Paul Anka, which became his signature song.
Nat King Cole became the first African American to host a national TV variety show, 'The Nat King Cole Show.'
Chuck Berry died in St. Charles County, Missouri, at age 90.
Little Richard died in Tullahoma, Tennessee, at age 87.
Jerry Lee Lewis died in Memphis, Tennessee, at age 87.
Loretta Lynn released 'Coal Miner's Daughter,' telling her autobiographical story of growing up in poverty.
Loretta Lynn died at her ranch in Hurricane Mills, Tennessee, at age 90.
George Jones recorded 'He Stopped Loving Her Today,' often called the greatest country song ever.
The O Brother, Where Art Thou? soundtrack won Album of the Year at the Grammys, reviving interest in bluegrass and folk.
Kris Kristofferson landed a helicopter in Johnny Cash's yard to deliver a demo tape, launching his songwriting career.
Chris Stapleton swept the CMA Awards, winning Male Vocalist, New Artist, and Album of the Year.
Morgan Wallen's album sales surged after a racial slur controversy, exposing tensions in country music.
Lil Nas X's 'Old Town Road' was removed from Billboard's country chart, sparking debate about genre boundaries and race.
Beyoncé released 'Cowboy Carter,' her country-influenced album, debuting at #1 on the Billboard 200 and Country chart.
Charley Pride became the first Black country music superstar, winning CMA Entertainer of the Year.
Charley Pride died of COVID-19 complications in Dallas at age 86.
George Strait holds the record for most #1 country singles with 60.
Taylor Swift's 'Tim McGraw,' her debut single, was released when she was 16.
Garth Brooks became the first artist to have seven albums debut at #1 on the Billboard 200 (at the time).
The Carter Family's 'Can the Circle Be Unbroken' was recorded, becoming one of the foundational country/folk songs.
Woody Guthrie died of Huntington's disease in New York at age 55.
Pete Seeger died in New York at age 94.
Nina Simone performed 'Mississippi Goddam' live for the first time, a direct response to the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing.
Sam & Dave recorded 'Soul Man' at Stax Records in Memphis.
Isaac Hayes released 'Hot Buttered Soul,' pioneering long-form soul with orchestral arrangements.
Curtis Mayfield released 'Superfly,' a soundtrack that outperformed the film and defined blaxploitation-era soul.
Earth, Wind & Fire's 'September' was released, becoming one of the most enduring and joyful songs in music history.
Luther Vandross released 'Never Too Much,' launching his career as one of R&B's greatest vocalists.
Erykah Badu released 'Baduizm,' pioneering the neo-soul movement.
Lauryn Hill performed 'Doo Wop (That Thing)' on Saturday Night Live while visibly pregnant.
Usher released 'Confessions,' which sold over 20 million copies and produced four #1 singles.
Frank Ocean released 'Blonde,' a surprise album that debuted at #1 and redefined alternative R&B.
H.E.R. won Song of the Year at the Grammys for 'I Can't Breathe,' inspired by the Black Lives Matter movement.
The first Tomorrowland electronic music festival was held in Boom, Belgium.
Swedish House Mafia performed their farewell show at Ultra Music Festival in Miami.
Calvin Harris became the first artist to have 10 songs surpass 100 million streams each on Spotify.
Marshmello performed an in-game concert in Fortnite, watched by over 10 million players simultaneously.
Travis Scott's 'Astronomical' Fortnite concert was attended by 12.3 million concurrent players.
ABBA reunited and released 'Voyage,' their first album in 40 years.
Elton John performed his final North American concert at Dodger Stadium, 48 years after his iconic 1975 shows there.
Paul McCartney headlined Glastonbury at age 80, the oldest solo headliner in the festival's history.
Rihanna performed the Super Bowl LVII halftime show, her first live performance in over five years, while revealing her pregnancy.
Sinéad O'Connor died at her home in London at age 56.
Tina Turner's 'What's Love Got to Do with It' won three Grammys and became her biggest solo hit.
The first use of Auto-Tune as a vocal effect was on Cher's 'Believe,' creating a robotic vocal effect that dominated pop for decades.
T-Pain popularized Auto-Tune in hip-hop with 'Buy U a Drank,' making it a defining sound of late-2000s pop.
Björk released 'Homogenic,' a volcanic fusion of electronic beats and orchestral strings.
Nine Inch Nails' Trent Reznor won the Academy Award for Best Original Score for 'The Social Network,' with Atticus Ross.
Hans Zimmer's score for 'Inception' popularized the 'BRAAAM' sound effect that dominated movie trailers for a decade.
Lin-Manuel Miranda's 'Hamilton' opened on Broadway, blending hip-hop with historical narrative and winning 11 Tonys.
The Jonas Brothers reunited after a six-year hiatus, releasing 'Sucker' which debuted at #1.
One Direction was formed on The X Factor from five solo auditioners: Harry Styles, Zayn Malik, Niall Horan, Liam Payne, and Louis Tomlinson.
Zayn Malik left One Direction, devastating fans worldwide.
Liam Payne died after falling from a hotel balcony in Buenos Aires, Argentina, at age 31.
Harry Styles' 'As It Was' spent 15 weeks at #1 on the Billboard Hot 100.
Miley Cyrus performed a controversial VMA performance with Robin Thicke, sparking widespread debate.
Ariana Grande's 'Thank U, Next' broke the record for biggest Spotify debut with 8.3 million streams in its first day.
A bomb exploded at the Manchester Arena during an Ariana Grande concert, killing 22 people.
Ariana Grande organized the One Love Manchester benefit concert two weeks after the bombing.
The Station nightclub fire in West Warwick, Rhode Island, killed 100 people during a Great White concert.
Billie Eilish recorded 'When We All Fall Asleep, Where Do We Go?' in her brother Finneas's bedroom.
Olivia Rodrigo's 'SOUR' debuted at #1 and produced three top-10 singles.
Doja Cat's 'Say So' topped the Hot 100 after a TikTok dance trend.
TikTok became the primary driver of music discovery, with the platform responsible for breaking numerous hit songs.
Ice Spice's 'Munch' went viral on TikTok, launching her career.
The Recording Academy expanded the Grammy nominations from 5 to 8-10 in major categories.
Kanye West legally changed his name to Ye.
Kanye West's antisemitic remarks led to his music being pulled from some platforms and losing business partnerships.
R. Kelly was convicted of sex trafficking and racketeering.
Phil Spector was convicted of murder and sentenced to 19 years to life.
Phil Spector died in prison at age 81.
George Michael died on Christmas Day at his home in Oxfordshire, England, at age 53.
Tom Petty died of an accidental drug overdose in Los Angeles at age 66.
Chris Cornell of Soundgarden died by suicide in Detroit at age 52.
Scott Weiland of Stone Temple Pilots was found dead on his tour bus in Minnesota at age 48.
Layne Staley of Alice in Chains was found dead in his Seattle apartment at age 34.
Shannon Hoon of Blind Melon was found dead on his tour bus in New Orleans at age 28.
Bradley Nowell of Sublime died of a heroin overdose in a San Francisco hotel at age 28.
Left Eye of TLC was killed in a car crash in Honduras at age 30.
Aaliyah died in a plane crash in the Bahamas at age 22.
Ol' Dirty Bastard of Wu-Tang Clan died of an accidental drug overdose in New York at age 35.
MF DOOM died on October 31 but his death was not announced until December 31.
Pop Smoke was shot and killed during a home invasion in Los Angeles at age 20.
Young Dolph was shot and killed at a cookie shop in Memphis at age 36.
Takeoff of Migos was shot and killed in Houston at age 28.
Michael Jackson's Neverland Ranch was raided by police as part of child molestation investigation.
Woodstock '99 devolved into fires, riots, and assaults, marking the end of an era for large-scale rock festivals.
The Fyre Festival, promoted by Ja Rule and Billy McFarland, was exposed as a massive fraud.
Metallica sued Napster for copyright infringement, becoming a focal point in the debate over digital music piracy.
Prince changed his name to an unpronounceable symbol in a dispute with Warner Bros. Records.
Taylor Swift wrote an open letter to Apple Music, forcing them to pay artists during the free trial period.
Dolly Parton was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame despite initially declining.
Led Zeppelin won a copyright infringement case over the 'Stairway to Heaven' riff, which had been challenged as plagiarism of Spirit's 'Taurus.'
